briefing.opening: Good morning. I will now begin the climate briefing for March 31, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Domestic and International Climate Crisis Phenomena and Policy Difficulties
sections.content: Domestically, there is a possibility that cherry blossom blooming in March will become entrenched due to the rapid northward advance of spring flowers [2], and the Gwangju and Jeonnam regions have been directly hit by a series of meteorological disasters including heavy rainfall, wildfires, and heatwaves [6]. Internationally, winter warming is anticipated in the Western United States in 2026 [55], and reports that global climate change has entered a 'red zone' [73] are intensifying the sense of crisis. Amidst this crisis, news that the introduction of carbon pricing, a key climate policy, is facing difficulties due to political barriers [5] raises concerns. Furthermore, while 4 trillion won was allocated for oil price support in the government's supplementary budget, only 500 billion won was assigned for renewable energy transition [40], leading to the publication of feature articles on the potential retreat of carbon neutrality policies [11].

sections.title: ⚠️ Increasing Threats to Ecosystems and Human Health
sections.content: Climate change continues to be reported as having severe impacts across ecosystems. Canada's marine economy is threatened by climate change [53], and research suggests that 'fast-food' phytoplankton could be created alongside the biochemical reorganization of phytoplankton cell composition [56, 52]. Over 200 species, including Australia's superb fairy-wren [74, 75] and raptors in the UK [76], are facing extinction, and the destruction of biodiversity is accelerating, with mass oyster mortality observed in Japan [78]. Human health is also directly threatened; unhealthy air could become commonplace for 100 million Americans by 2100 [60], and research also suggests that climate change can worsen seasonal allergies and even lead to an increase in suicides [66].

sections.title: 🌱 Expanding Domestic and International Efforts and Citizen Participation for Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Various efforts to achieve carbon neutrality are ongoing in several local governments across the country, with active movements encouraging citizen participation. Seongnam City [7, 12, 14], Yecheon County [10, 29, 31], and Yangju City Comprehensive Welfare Center for the Disabled [32], among others, are striving to raise awareness by operating carbon neutrality education programs for seniors and employees. Everyday carbon neutrality practices are also spreading, such as Nonsan City's Arbor Day tree planting [15], Hwasun County's pilot operation of eco-friendly funeral homes [18], and Yeongju City's campaign to use domestic timber [24]. Notably, Siheung City [19], Osan City [21], and Cheongju City in Chungcheongbuk-do [23] are encouraging active citizen climate action through 'Climate Action Opportunity Income' and 'Carbon Neutrality Point Systems', and Busan City is proposing a 'Busan-type Carbon Credit Model' [27], highlighting the proactive policy implementation by local governments. Internationally, the 'Green Deal Call Project' is underway in Europe, promoting resilient, sustainable, and climate-neutral systemic changes [72].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and New Investment Trends
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is showing significant progress both domestically and internationally, with expanding investments. Airrane, Seoul City Gas, and GRES have signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) to accelerate carbon-neutral energy projects in the Seoul metropolitan area [8, 25], and the Ministry of Climate and the Ministry of Industry requested a supplementary budget, including securing carbon-neutral technologies for the steel industry, with 524.5 billion won increased in the Ministry of Climate's supplementary budget for renewable energy finance and supply support [9, 47]. In Jeju Island, energy governance has been launched, initiating the realization of carbon neutrality by 2035 [16, 22], and the transition is accelerating with the prospect of offshore wind supergrids gaining momentum [36] and Korea Electric Power Corporation (KEPCO) resuming reviews for new renewable energy generation permits [42]. Furthermore, legal grounds have been established to support resident-led renewable energy cooperatives [41], and globally, innovation and investment in the renewable energy sector are active, with U.S. renewable energy startups attracting large-scale investments for a clean energy future in 2026 [48].

sections.title: 🌍 International Legal and Social Discussions for Climate Change Response
sections.content: International legal and social discussions for climate change response are also actively unfolding. In Vermont and Maine, discussions and research are underway regarding legislation that would require fossil fuel companies and polluters to pay for climate change costs [50, 57]. Information on the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) is being presented [54], and the importance of the nexus between climate change, migration, and health is being emphasized [58]. The emergence of 'eco-chaplains,' new spiritual care providers for those coping with climate change [59], is an interesting phenomenon that demonstrates how the climate crisis demands societal-wide changes. The news that Professor Carl Wunsch received an award for identifying the impacts of ocean warming [68] once again reminds us of the importance of climate science research.

briefing.closing: As we have seen in today's briefing, the climate crisis is having a comprehensive impact on our society, and various domestic and international response efforts are simultaneously underway. Only sustained attention and action can create a better future. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: “Domestic Winter Sports Industry Must Overcome Crisis by Attracting Young People Amidst Slump”
articles.original_title: “국내 동계스포츠 산업 침체 국면 젊은층 유입으로 위기 극복해야”
articles.description: Professor Seok Kang-hoon proposes sustainability: "New demand sharply declining due to cost and accessibility; introduce artificial slopes like Japan." The domestic winter sports industry is entering a structural slump. Concerns are rising that the entire winter sports industry ecosystem, centered around ski resorts, is being shaken by a combination of shortened seasons due to climate change, rising costs, and diversifying leisure activities. Seok Kang-hoon, Chairman of the Korea Ski and Snowboard Association's Sports for All Committee (National Han...

summary.phenomenon: The domestic winter sports industry is experiencing a structural slump, with new demand sharply declining due to factors such as cost and accessibility. The industry ecosystem, particularly ski resorts, is facing instability due to shortened seasons caused by climate change, rising operational costs, and a diversification of leisure activities.
summary.cause: The slump is attributed to shortened seasons resulting from climate change, increasing operational costs, and the diversification of leisure activities, which collectively reduce new demand.
summary.outlook: To overcome this crisis, it is suggested that the industry must attract younger demographics and consider introducing innovations such as artificial slopes, similar to approaches taken in Japan.

articles.original_description: 석강훈 교수, 지속 가능성 제안“비용·접근성 이유 신규 수요 급감 일본처럼 인공 슬로프 등 도입을”국내 동계스포츠 산업이 구조적 침체 국면에 접어들고 있다. 기후변화에 따른 시즌 단축과 비용 상승, 여가 다양화 흐름이 맞물리며 스키장을 중심으로 한 동계스포츠 산업 생태계 전반이 흔들리고 있다는 우려가 나온다.석강훈 대한스키스노보드협회 생활체육위원장(국립한···

articles.description: Merlin could disappear in worst-case scenario, with British isles facing ecological ‘point of no return’The merlin, Britain’s smallest bird of prey, is one of more than 200 species that will become extinct in the UK if action is not taken to curb emissions and unsustainable land use, a study has claimed.According to the UK Centre for Ecology & Hydrology (UKCEH), there is a 20-year window in which decisions on climate and land use will determine the fate of dozens of Britain’s native species.
